decision: Whether to use raw event streaming (stream=True with manual event parsing) or the SDK's simplified text_stream interface when building a chat UI
context: A team is building a customer-facing chat interface where responses must appear word-by-word. A junior engineer suggests parsing raw MessageStart, ContentBlockDelta, and MessageStop events manually so they can log every event type for debugging. A senior engineer proposes using client.messages.stream() with text_stream instead.
options: Use stream=True and manually iterate over all event types, filtering for ContentBlockDelta to extract text
Use client.messages.stream() with the text_stream iterator, which automatically extracts only text chunks
Use standard non-streaming messages.create() and display the full response once complete
correct: Option B (text_stream) is best for chat UI display. The SDK's simplified interface automatically filters to text content, eliminating boilerplate event-type branching and reducing the surface area for bugs where a developer accidentally renders tool-use or metadata events as visible text.
constraint: When the production requirement is purely rendering text to users in real time and there is no need to act on non-text event types during the stream
failure_mode: Manual event parsing that fails to filter non-text event types can cause raw JSON metadata (ContentBlockStart, MessageDelta payloads) to appear in the user-facing UI, breaking the chat experience

decision: Whether to discard the streamed chunks after display or capture the assembled final message object for storage and downstream processing
context: A production chat application streams Claude's response to the browser as it arrives. After the stream ends, the application must persist the full conversation turn to a database and pass the complete response text to a downstream sentiment-analysis service. The engineer wonders whether to reconstruct the message by concatenating all streamed chunks in-memory or to use the SDK's get_final_message() method after streaming.
options: Concatenate each text chunk in a local string variable during streaming and use that as the stored response
After the streaming context manager exits, call stream.get_final_message() to obtain the fully assembled message object including usage metadata
Make a second non-streaming API call after streaming finishes to retrieve a clean copy of the response
correct: Option B is correct. get_final_message() returns the complete assembled Message object with accurate token usage counts and all content blocks, which is required for cost attribution and reliable storage without a second billable API call.
constraint: When the application must both stream for UX and store the complete response with accurate usage metadata for cost tracking or audit purposes
failure_mode: Manually concatenated chunks lack token usage data (input_tokens, output_tokens), causing cost-reporting dashboards to show zero or incorrect usage; a second redundant API call doubles cost and latency for every turn

decision: Whether to stream Claude's response directly to the end-user client or buffer the complete response server-side before forwarding, when the server must validate or redact content before display
context: A legal-services platform uses Claude to draft preliminary contract clauses. Compliance policy requires that every response pass through a PII redaction filter and a legal-disclaimer injection step before reaching end users. The engineering team is debating whether to use streaming end-to-end (server → Claude → client) or to stream from Claude to the server, buffer the full text, post-process it, and then send the final version to the client.
options: Stream chunks directly from Claude through the server to the client in real time, applying regex redaction on each chunk before forwarding
Stream from Claude to the server, buffer all chunks into a complete response, apply full-document redaction and disclaimer injection, then send the final text to the client in one payload
Disable streaming entirely and use standard messages.create() to simplify the post-processing pipeline
correct: Option B is correct. Chunk-level regex redaction (Option A) is unreliable because PII such as a full name or account number can span a chunk boundary, causing partial tokens to slip through undetected. Option C sacrifices all perceived-latency benefit. Option B preserves server-side streaming efficiency while guaranteeing that compliance filters operate on complete, coherent text.
constraint: When compliance or safety rules require whole-document context to correctly identify and transform content before it reaches the user, making partial-chunk processing semantically unsafe
failure_mode: Chunk-boundary PII leakage: a name split across two chunks ('Jo' + 'hn Smith') causes the redaction regex to miss the pattern in both chunks, exposing regulated data to the end user and creating a compliance violation

decision: Whether to set max_tokens conservatively (e.g., 256) or generously (e.g., 4096) when enabling streaming for a chat application where response length is unpredictable
context: A developer is enabling streaming for a general-purpose assistant that handles both quick factual questions and requests for long-form explanations. They are setting max_tokens for the streaming call and want to balance responsiveness, cost, and completeness.
options: Set max_tokens=256 to keep responses short and fast for all query types
Set max_tokens=4096 (or the model's practical limit) to ensure long responses are never truncated mid-stream
Omit max_tokens entirely and rely on the model's default behavior
correct: Option B is correct for a general-purpose assistant. Streaming masks latency for the user, so the cost of generating a long response is perceptually low. A low max_tokens causes the stream to cut off mid-sentence for complex queries, which is more disruptive when users are watching text appear in real time. Omitting max_tokens (Option C) risks unbounded generation and unpredictable costs.
constraint: When streaming is active and query length is unpredictable, truncation mid-stream is more damaging to UX than truncation in a buffered response because users watch the sentence stop abruptly
failure_mode: With max_tokens=256 on a streaming chat, users see Claude's explanation cut off mid-sentence in real time—a highly visible failure that erodes trust—while complex queries silently return incomplete answers that users may act on as if complete
